5. What is low fedility and high fedility  
design ?  
A low-fidelity design is a simple sketch or wireframe that focuses on layout, structure, and  
user flow rather than colors, images, or visual details.  
High-fidelity design is a detailed, pixel-perfect representation of the final product, including  
colors, typography, images, and interactions, used before development and for stakeholder  
approval.  
6. what is ui & ux ? what are the difference  
between ui developer and ux developer?  
UI (User Interface) is the visual part of a website or application that users interact with.  
It includes:  
● Buttons  
● Forms  
● Navigation menus  
● Colors  
● Typography  
● Icons  
● Layouts  
● Animations  
Example  
In an e-commerce website:  
● Product cards  
● Add to Cart button  
● Search bar  
● Header and Footer  
These are all part of the UI.  
UX (User Experience) focuses on how users feel when using a product and how easily they  
can achieve their goals.  
It includes:  
● User research  
● User journey mapping  
● Information architecture  
● Wireframing  
● Usability testing  
● Accessibility  
● User flows  
Example  
In an e-commerce website:  
● Can users find products quickly?  
● Is checkout simple?  
● How many steps are needed to place an order?  
● Are users getting confused?  
These are UX concerns.  
A UI Developer converts designs into working web pages and applications.  
Responsibilities  
● Build responsive layouts  
● Write HTML, CSS, JavaScript  
● Develop React/Next.js components  
● Implement animations  
● Ensure cross-browser compatibility  
● Follow design specifications  
while a UX Designer researches user needs, creates wireframes, designs user flows, and  
improves the overall user experience.  
Before building an e-commerce checkout page, the UX Designer determines:  
● How many checkout steps should exist?  
● What information should be collected?  
● Where users typically abandon the process?  
7. how to make a responsive website  
using tailwind css?  
Tailwind CSS is mobile-first, which means you design for mobile devices first and then add  
styles for larger screens using responsive breakpoints.  
Tailwind Breakpoints  
640px and above  
768px and above  
1024px and bove  
1280px and bove  
1536px and bove  
sm:  
md:  
lg:  
xl:  
2xl:  
8. What is Design Tokens?  
Design Tokens are reusable variables that store design values such as colors, typography,  
spacing, border radius, shadows, and breakpoints.  
1. Color Tokens  
{
"color": {  
"primary": "#2563EB",  
"secondary": "#64748B",  
"success": "#22C55E",  
"danger": "#EF4444"  
}
}
Usage:  
background-color: var(--color-primary);  
9. What is the rules to create profesional  
design.  
1. Follow Visual Hierarchy  
2. Keep the Design Simple  
3. Maintain Consistency  
4. Use Proper White Space  
5. Follow an 8px Spacing System  
7. Use Good Typography (Choose 1–2 font families. )  
8. Design on a Grid  
10. Create Reusable Components  
14. Use Design Tokens  
9. What is Semantic HTML?  
Semantic HTML means using HTML elements according to their meaning and purpose, rather  
than just for styling or layout.  
Semantic tags clearly describe the content they contain, making the code easier for developers,  
browsers, search engines, and assistive technologies to understand.  
10. What is DOM (Document Object  
Model)?  
The DOM is a tree-like representation of an HTML document created by the browser.  
JavaScript uses the DOM to read, modify, add, or remove elements from a webpage.  
11. What is BOM (Browser Object Model)?  
The BOM allows JavaScript to interact with the browser itself, not just the webpage.  
The BOM provides objects like:  
● window  
● location  
● history  
● navigator  
● screen  
● localStorage  
● sessionStorage  
12. What is Shadow DOM?  
The Shadow DOM is a special DOM that is isolated from the main DOM.  
It is mainly used in:  
● Web Components  
● Custom Elements  
● UI Libraries  
It helps prevent CSS and JavaScript conflicts.  
Why Shadow DOM?  
Imagine your website has:  
button {  
background: red;  
}
But a third-party component wants:  
button {  
background: blue;  
}
Without Shadow DOM, styles can conflict.  
With Shadow DOM, the component's styles stay isolated.  
Shadow DOM Example  
<custom-button></custom-button>  
JavaScript:  
const shadow =  
element.attachShadow({ mode: "open" });  
shadow.innerHTML = `  
<style>  
button {  
background: blue;  
color: white;  
}
</style>  
<button>Buy Now</button>  
`;  
The styles inside the Shadow DOM won't affect the rest of the page.  
Shadow DOM Structure  
Document  
└── BODY  
└── custom-button  
└── Shadow Root  
└── BUTTON  
The button exists inside its own isolated DOM tree.  
13. what is primitive and non primitive  
data types  
Primitive Data Types  
Primitive data types store a single value directly in memory. They are immutable (cannot  
be changed directly).  
There are 7 primitive data types in JavaScript:  
Data Type  
String  
Example  
"Hello"  
Number  
Boolean  
100, 3.14  
true, false  
let x;  
Undefine  
d
Null  
let y = null;  
Symbol("id")  
Symbol  
BigInt  
12345678901234567  
890n  
Example  
let name = "Bicky";  
let age = 34;  
let isDeveloper = true;  
Characteristics  
Stores actual value directly  
Immutable  
Compared by value  
let a = 10;  
let b = 10;  
console.log(a === b); // true  
Non-primitive data types store references (memory addresses) instead of the actual value.  
Main non-primitive types:  
Data Type  
Example  
Object  
{ name: "Bicky"  
}
Array  
[1, 2, 3]  
Function  
function  
hello() {}  
Date  
Map  
Set  
new Date()  
new Map()  
new Set()  
Example  
let user = {  
name: "Bicky",  
age: 34  
};  
Characteristics  
Stores reference (memory address)  
Mutable  
Compared by reference  
let obj1 = { name: "Bicky" };  
let obj2 = { name: "Bicky" };  
console.log(obj1 === obj2); // false  
Even though both objects contain the same data, they occupy different memory locations.  
14. what is UseState?  
useStateis a React Hook used to add state to functional components. It returns an array  
containing the current state value and a setter function to update that value. When the state  
changes using the setter function, React automatically re-renders the component and updates  
the UI.  
15. what is UseEffect?  
useEffectis a React Hook used to perform side effects in a component.A side effect is  
anything that happens outside of rendering UI, such as:  
● Fetching API data  
● Calling a backend service  
● Setting a timer  
● Adding event listeners  
● Updating the document title  
15. What is useMemo?  
useMemo is a React Hook used to cache (memorize) the result of an  
expensive calculation so it doesn't run on every render.  
const memoizedValue = useMemo(() => {  
return expensiveCalculation();  
}, [dependency]);  
const total = useMemo(() => count * 2, [count]);  
useMemo remembers a calculated value and recalculates it only when  
needed.  
16. What is useCallback?  
useCallback is a React Hook used to cache (memorize) a function so  
React doesn't create a new function on every render.  
const memoizedFunction = useCallback(() => {  
// function code  
}, [dependency]);  
const addToCart = useCallback((product) => {  
dispatch(addProduct(product));  
}, []);  
Or  
const handleClick = useCallback(() => {  
console.log("clicked");  
}, []);  
useCallback remembers a function and recreates it only when needed.  
17. What is Vertual DOM?  
Virtual DOM (VDOM) is a lightweight copy of the real DOM that React  
keeps in memory.  
Instead of updating the browser's DOM directly, React:  
1. Creates a Virtual DOM.  
2. Detects what changed.  
3. Updates only the changed parts in the Real DOM.  
This makes React fast and efficient.  
18. How to optimise react application?  
1. Use useMemo()  
const totalPrice = useMemo(() => {  
return products.reduce((sum, p) => sum + p.price, 0);  
}, [products]);  
Use when: Heavy calculations are causing slow renders.  
2. Use useCallback()  
Cache functions to prevent unnecessary re-renders of child components.  
const handleAddToCart = useCallback(() => {  
console.log("Added");  
}, []);  
Use when: Passing functions as props to child components.  
4. Code Splitting & Lazy Loading  
Load components only when needed.  
import { lazy, Suspense } from "react";  
const ProductPage = lazy(() => import("./ProductPage"));  
<Suspense fallback={<div>Loading...</div>}>  
<ProductPage />  
</Suspense>;  
This reduces the initial bundle size.  
5. Optimize Images  
<img  
src="image.webp"  
loading="lazy"  
alt="Product"  
/>  
6. Debounce Search Inputs  
Avoid API calls on every keystroke.  
const debouncedSearch = debounce(searchUsers, 500);  
Example:  
● User types "React"  
● API called once after typing stops  
Not 5 times.  
7. Optimize API Calls  
Use caching libraries such as:  
● TanStack Query  
● SWR  
Benefits:  
● Caching  
● Background refetching  
● Deduplication  
● Better performance  
7. Keep State Close to Where It's Needed  
19. what is useContext?  
useContextis a React Hook used to share data between components  
without passing props manually at every level.  
It helps solve Prop Drilling.  
20. How to change the Bootstrap CSS  
default color?  
a. Override Bootstrap CSS (Quick Method)  
.btn-primary {  
background-color: #ff5722;  
border-color: #ff5722;  
}
.btn-primary:hover {  
background-color: #e64a19;  
}
b. Use CSS Variables (Bootstrap 5)  
:root {  
--bs-primary: #ff5722;  
}
c. Change Bootstrap SCSS Variables (Best Practice)  
Before Importing Bootstrap  
$primary: #ff5722;  
$secondary: #009688;  
$success: #4caf50;  
$danger: #f44336;  
@import "bootstrap";  
Bootstrap will generate all button, alert, badge, and utility classes using your custom colors.  
$theme-colors: (  
"primary": #ff5722,  
"secondary": #607d8b,  
"success": #4caf50,  
"danger": #f44336,  
"warning": #ffc107,  
"info": #2196f3  
);  
Change multiple theme color.  
21. what is persona?  
A Persona is a fictional representation of your target user, created based  
on research, data, and assumptions.  
It helps designers, developers, and product teams understand:  
● Who the users are  
● What they need  
● Their goals  
● Their frustrations  
● Their behavior  
Simple Example  
Suppose you're building a Local Tutor App (Localy).  
Persona  
Name: Rahul Sharma  
Age: 32  
Occupation: Working Professional  
Location: Kolkata  
Goals  
● Find a tutor near his home.  
● Book demo classes easily.  
● Compare tutor ratings.  
Pain Points  
● Difficult to find trusted tutors.  
● Too many fake listings.  
● No easy way to compare tutors.  
Tech Skill  
● Uses mobile apps daily.  
● Comfortable with online payments.  
22. What is Mounting and Unmounting in  
React?  
Mounting = Component Enters the Screen  
When a component is created and displayed in the browser for the first  
time, it is called Mounting.  
Unmounting = Component Leaves the Screen  
When a component is removed from the browser screen, it is called Unmounting.  
Example:  
● Navigate from Home page to About page.  
● Home component is removed.  
● Home component is unmounted.  
Mounting  
● User opens Product Details page.  
● API call runs.  
● Product data loads.  
Unmounting  
● User leaves the page.  
● Cleanup runs.  
● Timers, subscriptions, or listeners are removed.  
Why is Unmounting Important?  
To prevent:  
● Memory leaks  
● Unnecessary API calls  
● Running timers in the background  
● Event listeners staying active  
23. what is vibe coading?  
Vibe Coding is a modern way of building software where you describe  
what you want in plain English and an AI generates most of the code for  
you.  
Instead of writing every line manually, you focus on:  
● The idea  
● The features  
● The user experience  
And the AI writes the implementation.  
24. What is Artificial Intelligence (AI)?  
Artiﬁcial Intelligence (AI) is a technology that enables machines and  
computers to perform tasks that typically require human intelligence. It  
allows systems to learn from data, recognise patterns, and make decisions to  
solve complex problems.  
Example: ChatGPT, self-driving cars, Google Translate.  
25. What is LLM? How do LLMs generate  
responses?  
LLM (Large Language Model) is an AI model trained on huge amounts of text data.  
It generates responses by:  
1. Reading your prompt.  
2. Breaking text into tokens.  
3. Predicting the most likely next token.  
4. Repeating the process until a complete answer is formed.  
Example:  
Input:  
React is a JavaScript _____  
LLM predicts:  
library  
26. Difference Between Artificial Intelligence  
vs Machine Learning vs Deep Learning?  
Artificial Intelligence (AI), Machine Learning (ML), and Deep Learning (DL) are closely related  
technologies that enable machines to perform intelligent tasks. AI is the broader concept of  
creating intelligent systems, ML is a subset of AI that learns from data, and DL is a specialized  
subset of ML that uses neural networks to learn complex patterns.  
27. What are Hallucinations?  
Hallucinations occur when an AI model generates information that sounds correct and  
confident but is actually false, inaccurate, or completely made up.  
Why Do Hallucinations Happen?  
1. Incomplete training data  
2. Ambiguous questions  
3. Lack of up-to-date information  
4. The model predicts likely text, not verified facts  
5. Missing context  
How to Reduce Hallucinations?  
● Verify information from official documentation.  
● Cross-check code examples.  
● Use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).  
● Provide clear and detailed prompts.  
● Ask the model for sources or references.  
● Test generated code before using it in production.  
28. What is a Context Window?  
A context window is the amount of information (measured in tokens) that an AI model can  
remember and use at one time while generating a response.  
Think of it as the AI's short-term memory during a conversation.  
Simple Example  
Imagine you're talking to a friend.  
You say:  
My name is Bicky.  
Later you ask:  
What's my name?  
Your friend remembers because it was mentioned recently.  
Similarly, an AI remembers information that fits within its context window.  
29. What is a Token?  
In AI (especially Large Language Models like ChatGPT), a token is a small unit of text.  
A token can be:  
● A word  
● Part of a word  
● A punctuation mark  
● A number  
● A symbol  
Example  
Text:  
I love React.  
May be split into tokens like:  
["I", " love", " React", "."]  
30. What is CRISP Prompting?  
CRISP Prompting is a framework for writing clear and effective AI prompts so the AI  
produces more accurate, structured, and useful responses.  
Each letter represents an important part of a good prompt.  
Example:  
Context: I am building a Figma Clone using Next.js 16.  
Role: Act as a Senior Frontend Architect.  
Instructions: Create a responsive dashboard layout with authentication and protected  
routes.  
Specifics: Use TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, App Router, reusable components, clean folder  
structure, and accessibility best practices. Include comments where necessary.  
Purpose: The project should be scalable, maintainable, and suitable for a production  
application.  
31. What is CRISP Prompting?  
A good prompt is clear, specific, and provides enough context for the AI to understand  
exactly what you want.  
A bad prompt is vague, incomplete, or ambiguous, forcing the AI to guess your intent.  
Bad Prompt  
Create a login page.  
Good Prompt  
Act as a Senior React Developer. Create a responsive login page using React,  
TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS. Include email and password fields, form  
validation, error messages, a loading state, and accessibility best practices.  
Write clean, reusable code.  
32. What is Prompt Engineering?  
Prompt Engineering is the skill of writing clear, structured, and effective prompts so an  
AI model produces accurate, relevant, and high-quality responses.  
33. What is Hallucination in AI?  
Hallucination is when an AI generates incorrect information confidently. It is predicting the most  
likely response based on patterns, and sometimes those predictions are wrong.  
Why Does Hallucination Happen?  
Some common reasons are:  
● The incomplete prompt.  
● The AI lacks enough context.  
● The requested information wasn't part of its training or context.  
● The AI tries to fill in missing information instead of saying "I don't know."  
1. Write Clear Prompts  
Bad Prompt  
Explain React.  
Good Prompt  
Explain React Hooks with examples using React 19 and TypeScript.  
Provide Context  
Instead of:  
Fix my code.  
Write:  
I'm using React 19, TypeScript, and Vite. Here's the component and the error  
message.  
Break Large Tasks into Smaller Steps  
Instead of:  
Build a complete e-commerce website.  
Try:  
1. Create project structure.  
2. Build authentication.  
3. Create product listing.  
4. Add cart.  
5. Integrate payments.  
This reduces ambiguity.  
34. What is Hallucination in AI?  
MCP stands for Model Context Protocol.  
An MCP Server is a server that allows an AI model (like ChatGPT or Claude) to securely  
connect to external tools, data sources, and services using a standard protocol.  
Think of it as a bridge between an AI and external systems.  
Real-Life Analogy  
Imagine you're a frontend developer.  
Normally, if you ask an AI:  
"Show me all the users in my database."  
The AI cannot access your database directly.  
But if your company has an MCP Server connected to the database:  
You  
│
▼
ChatGPT / Claude  
│
▼
MCP Server  
│
▼
Database  
The AI can request the data through the MCP Server (subject to permissions).  
What Can an MCP Server Connect To?  
An MCP Server can expose tools such as:  
●
●
●
●
●
●
●
●
Local files  
Databases  
APIs  
GitHub repositories  
Google Drive  
Slack  
Google Calendar  
Custom business applications  
Why Use an MCP Server?  
Standard way for AI to connect to tools  
Secure access with permissions  
Reusable across different AI applications  
Easier integration with company systems  
35. Difference Between React and Next.js  
36. What is AI Agent and Agentic AI and  
Generative AI?  
Generative AI creates content based on your prompt.  
An AI Agent can use tools to complete a task.  
Agentic AI plans and completes an entire goal with minimal human guidance.  
37. What is CSR, SSR, SSG, and ISR in  
Next.js  
CSR (Client-Side Rendering)  
In CSR, the browser loads a basic HTML page first, then JavaScript fetches the data and  
renders the UI.  
2. SSR (Server-Side Rendering)  
In SSR, the server generates the HTML for every request before sending it to the browser.  
3. SSG (Static Site Generation)  
In SSG, pages are generated once during the build process and stored as static HTML.  
4. ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration)  
ISR combines the speed of SSG with the ability to update pages automatically.  
38. What is Hoisting in JavaScript?  
Hoisting is JavaScript's behavior of moving declarations to the top of their scope during  
the memory creation phase, before the code is executed.  
Important: Only declarations are hoisted, not initializations.  
38. Difference Between var, let, and  
const?  
var, let, and constare all used to declare variables in JavaScript, but they differ in  
scope, hoisting, reassignment, and redeclaration.
